elements.sent-2411-002: Connected and autonomous vehicles (CAVs) offload computationally intensive tasks to multi-access edge computing (MEC) servers via vehicle-to-infrastructure (V2I) communication, enabling applications within the vehicular metaverse, which transforms physical environment into the digital space enabling advanced analysis or predictive modeling.
elements.sent-2411-003: A core challenge is physical-to-virtual (P2V) synchronization through digital twins (DTs), reliant on MEC networks and ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC).
elements.sent-2411-004: To address this, we introduce radiance field (RF) delta video compression (RFDVC), which uses RF-encoder and RF-decoder architecture using distributed RFs as DTs storing photorealistic 3D urban scene in compressed form.
elements.sent-2411-005: This method extracts differences between CAV-frame capturing actual traffic and RF-frame capturing empty scene from the same pose in batches encoded and transmitted over the MEC network.
elements.sent-2411-006: Experiments show data savings up to 90\% against H.264 codec and 45\% against H.265 codec under different conditions as lighting changes, rain and fog.
elements.sent-2411-007: RFDVC exhibits greater resilience to transmission errors, achieving a bit error rate (BER) of approximately \(10^{-1.6}\) at signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of 10 dB, compared to \(10^{-1.4}\) for standard video coding.

elements.sent-2411-041: We present the foundational principles of the H.264 and H.265 codecs widely utilized for real-time video streaming to achieve efficient video compression via inter-frame and intra-frame encoding techniques.
elements.sent-2411-042: Subsequently, we introduce RFs as compressed representations of 3D scenes.
elements.sent-2411-043: Standard video codecs like H.264 and H.265 (HEVC) are critical for real-time video streaming, including applications in V2I communication.
elements.sent-2411-044: These codecs use spatial compression to eliminate redundancy within individual frames and temporal compression to minimize redundancy across multiple frames.
elements.sent-2411-045: Frames are generally organized into a group of pictures (GOP) structure, defined as the interval between two complete frames, known as intra-frames.
elements.sent-2411-046: The GOP consists of both intra-frames (I-frames) and inter-frames.
elements.sent-2411-047: I-frames store the full image information, similar to a standard jpeg file.
elements.sent-2411-048: Inter-frames, which depend on I-frames for reconstruction, are categorized into P-frames and B-frames.
elements.sent-2411-049: P-frames store differences from preceding frames, while B-frames store differences from both preceding and subsequent frames.
elements.sent-2411-050: B-frames achieve the highest compression and have the smallest file size among these frame types.
elements.sent-2411-051: Fig.~\ref{fig:cav_mec ~b) shows a sequence of encoded frames transmitted over the network within a GOP structure.}
elements.sent-2411-052: It highlights the arrangement of B-frames and P-frames between two I-frames.
elements.sent-2411-053: Extending the GOP length can reduce the overall data size by decreasing the number of I-frames, which are the largest contributors to the GOP size.
elements.sent-2411-054: However, this approach may reduce frame reconstruction quality and increase vulnerability to errors caused by network issues, such as packet loss or bit flips.
elements.sent-2411-055: To balance compression efficiency, video quality, and latency, careful management of I-frame transmission frequency, or even avoiding frequent I-frame transmission, is necessary to prevent network congestion.
elements.sent-2411-056: In the context of CAVs, maintaining low latency remains a crucial challenge, and the compression of I-frames is still a significant bottleneck in standard video coding (VC) when deployed for real-time data transmission.

elements.section-2411-004: Proposed Radiance Field Delta Video Compression
elements.sent-2411-084: Efficient video streaming is critical in CAVs for transmitting high-resolution frames from onboard cameras to MEC servers for subsequent processing.
elements.sent-2411-085: In this section, we propose RFDVC, a method aimed at minimizing latency and optimizing network performance by transmitting only novel or critical data, thereby excluding redundant and repetitive information.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figures/rfdvc.pdf} \caption{The proposed RFDVC utilizes distributed RFs within the MEC network. The diagram shows a) (a) RF-encoder extracts pairs of input CAV-frames and RF-frames to segment delta-frames using the DS algorithm. Delta-frames are then encoded via the H.264/H.265 codec and transmitted over the channel. (b) The RF-decoder subsequently decodes the delta and combines delta-frames with RF-frames to reconstruct rec-frames.} \label{fig:video_compression_rf_av}
elements.sent-2411-086: The proposed method employs an encoder-decoder architecture optimized for V2I communication between CAV and a MEC server, leveraging RFs distributed on both ends.
elements.sent-2411-087: On the CAV side (sender), we use a RF-encoder as depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:video_compression_rf_av ~a) The CAV is equipped with multiple onboard cameras that continuously capture the surrounding environment in real-time as CAV-frames.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Concurrently, a pretrained RF model generates RF-frames from the same camera poses, which represent an empty environment devoid of dynamic objects, such as vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-089: For each camera pose, we obtain a pair of frames: a CAV-frame reflecting the current traffic conditions and a RF-frame representing the static environment.
elements.sent-2411-090: To achieve this, we extract differences between image pairs to generate delta-frames.
elements.sent-2411-091: We employ a DS algorithm to process the CAV-frame and RF-frame pairs, producing delta-frames that are encoded using a standard video codecs.
elements.sent-2411-092: The distributed RF model serves as the I-frame (a complete frame), thus I-frame does not have to be transmitted, allowing only novel data along with the corresponding camera poses to be transmitted to the MEC server.
elements.sent-2411-093: Using the received camera poses, RF-frames are generated with the same RF model employed at the server side.
elements.sent-2411-094: Upon receiving the encoded delta-frames, the server decodes them and merges these delta-frames with the corresponding RF-frames to reconstruct the original CAV-frames, called rec-frames.
elements.sent-2411-095: Advanced DL techniques are then applied to the rec-frames to perform downstream tasks such as object detection, semantic segmentation or advanced scene understanding.
elements.sent-2411-096: In the RFEncoder procedure, lines $1\text{- 11$ iterate over each frame in CAV\_Frames, where the camera pose is extracted, RF-frame is rendered based on the pose, and the delta-frame is computed by comparing CAV-frame and RF-frame.}
elements.sent-2411-097: These computed delta-frames are accumulated in a list and subsequently encoded.
elements.sent-2411-098: The ChannelTX procedure, detailed in lines $12\text{- 19$, segments the encoded deltas for transmission according to the available network throughput, ensuring that data is sent efficiently with minimal delay.}
elements.sent-2411-099: During transmission, any lost packets are replaced with black pixels to maintain the frame structure and mitigate data loss.
elements.sent-2411-100: Finally, the RFDecoder procedure, represented by lines $20\text{- 31$, receives the transmitted deltas, decodes them, and reconstructs each frame by merging the decoded delta-frame with a freshly rendered RF-frame aligned to the transmitted pose, preserving frame integrity upon reception.}
elements.sent-2411-101: This three stage process reduces bandwidth by transmitting only delta information, thereby enabling efficient, high-quality frame reconstruction on the receiver side.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{RF-Encoder, Network Transmission, and RF-Decoder} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Procedure{RFEncoder}{$\mathbf{B}_{\text{cav}}, \Psi_{\text{rf}}$} \State $\mathbf{B}_{\Delta} \gets \text{empty list}$ \For{$F_{\text{cav}} \in \mathbf{B}_{\text{cav}}$} \State $\pi \gets \text{ExtractPose}(F_{\text{cav}})$ \State $F_{\text{rf}} \gets \text{RenderViewFromRF}(\Psi_{\text{rf}}, \pi)$ \State $F_{\Delta} \gets \text{SegDelta}(F_{\text{cav}}, F_{\text{rf}})$ \State $\text{Add}(\mathbf{B}_{\Delta}, (\pi, F_{\Delta}))$ \EndFor \State $\Delta \gets \text{EncDelta}(\mathbf{B}_{\Delta})$ \State return $\Delta$ \EndProcedure \label{alg:rf_encoder} \Procedure{ChannelTX}{$\Delta, \text{Th}$} \State $\mathbf{\Delta}_{tx}\gets \text{empty list}$ \State $|\Delta| \gets \text{GetSize}(\Delta)$ \State $\tau \gets |\Delta| / \text{Th}$ \State $\text{TransmitOverChannel}(\Delta, \tau)$ \State $\text{Add}(\mathbf{\Delta}_{tx}, (\Delta, \tau))$ \State return $\mathbf{\Delta}_{tx}$ \EndProcedure \label{alg:network_tx} \Procedure{RFDecoder}{$\mathbf{\Delta}_{tx}, \Psi_{\text{rf}}$} \State $\mathbf{B}_{rec}\gets \text{empty list}$ \State ($\Delta, \tau) \gets \text{GetData(}\mathbf{\Delta}_{tx})$ \State $\text{WaitForTransmission}(\tau)$ \State $\mathbf{B}_{\Delta} \gets \text{DecDelta}(\Delta)$ \For{each $(\pi, F_{\Delta}) \in \mathbf{B}_{\Delta}$} \State $F_{\text{rf}} \gets \text{RenderViewFromRF}(\Psi_{\text{rf}}, \pi)$ \State $F_{\text{rec}} \gets \text{RecCAVFrame}(F_{\text{rf}}, F_{\Delta})$ \State $\text{Add}(\mathbf{B}_{rec}, F_{\text{rec}})$ \EndFor \State return $\mathbf{B}_{rec}$ \EndProcedure \label{alg:rf_decoder} \end{algorithmic} \label{alg:pseudocode_rfdvc}

elements.sent-2411-109: The comparison between the masks is performed using the intersection over union (IoU) metric: \begin{equation}\text{IoU}(\mathcal{S}_{\text{cav}}, \mathcal{S}_{\text{rf}}) = \frac{ \lvert \mathcal{S}_{\text{cav}} \cap \mathcal{S}_{\text{rf}} \rvert }{ \lvert \mathcal{S}_{\text{cav}} \cup \mathcal{S}_{\text{rf}} \rvert },\end{equation} where $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ denotes the area of the masks.\par A match between masks is identified when the IoU exceeds a predefined threshold.

elements.sent-2411-166: In this work, we propose RFDVC approach, based on RF-encoder and RF-decoder architecture, for V2I communication and P2V synchronization within the vehicular metaverse.

elements.sent-2411-187: The main contributions of this paper are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-188: We present an RF-based encoder-decoder architecture that leverages distributed RFs as DTs for efficient and compressed 3D representation of urban scenes in the vehicular metaverse.
elements.sent-2411-189: We validate the data compression achieved by the RFDVC framework compared to H.264 and H.265 codecs across various environmental conditions, i.e., lighting, wet conditions, and rain.

elements.sent-2411-196: To systematically address the challenges of efficient data compression and transmission in vehicle-to-infrastructure (V2I) communication, we formalize the problem as an optimization task.
elements.sent-2411-197: The objective is to minimize the total amount of data transmitted while maintaining high-quality reconstruction of video frames and adhering to network constraints.
elements.sent-2411-198: This theoretical framework also helps establish an upper bound for achievable compression efficiency under ideal conditions, serving as a benchmark for evaluating the proposed RFDVC framework.
elements.sent-2411-199: In the V2I communication scenario, a connected autonomous vehicle (CAV) captures a batch of video frames, \( \mathcal{B _{\text{cav \), representing its current environment.}}}
elements.sent-2411-200: These frames are transmitted to a multi-access edge computing (MEC) server, where they are processed and used to synchronize physical-to-virtual (P2V) environments in the form of digital twins.
elements.sent-2411-201: Radiance fields (\( \Psi_{\text{rf \)) are used to model the static parts of the environment, and differences between the captured frames (\( F_{\text{cav \)) and the radiance field-generated frames (\( F_{\text{rf \)) are captured as Delta-frames (\( F_\Delta \)).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-202: Our objective is to reduce network communication overhead while ensuring that the transmitted frame batches preserve the scene semantics and information required for subsequent downstream tasks on MEC servers.
elements.sent-2411-203: The Delta-frame \( F_\Delta \) is computed as the pixel-wise difference between the captured frame \( F_{\text{cav \) and the radiance field frame \( F_{\text{rf \): \begin{equation}F_\Delta = F_{\text{cav}} - F_{\text{rf}}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-204: The sparsity of the Delta-frame (\( S_\Delta \)) refers to the total number of pixels not covered by the mask, as these represent the dynamic changes (e.g., moving objects) that must be transmitted.
elements.sent-2411-205: Sparsity is defined mathematically as: \begin{equation}S_\Delta = \sum_{p \in F_\Delta} \mathbb{I}(p \notin \text{mask}),\end{equation} where \( \mathbb{I (\cdot) \) is the indicator function.}
elements.sent-2411-206: The data transmitted, \( b_{\text{stream \), is the encoded version of the Delta-frames, where \( \eta(\cdot) \) represents the compression codec efficiency of used codec (e.g., H.264, H.265): \begin{equation}b_{\text{stream}} = \eta (F_\Delta).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-207: The main goal is to minimize the total data transmitted over the network, \( b_{\text{stream \), while ensuring high-quality reconstruction \( Q_{\text{rec (F_{\text{rec ) \) and complying with network constraints.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-208: The optimization problem is thus formulated as follows: \begin{equation}\min_{F_\Delta, b_{\text{stream}}} \|b_{\text{stream}}\|\end{equation} subject to:.

elements.sent-2411-210: The first pair of limiting constraints in practical scenarios are the throughput and latency.
elements.sent-2411-211: Specifically, the total transmitted data \( \|b_{\text{stream }\| \) must not exceed the available network throughput \( T_{\text{net \) multiplied by the allowable latency \( \tau \): \begin{equation}\|b_{\text{stream}}\| \leq T_{\text{net}} \cdot \tau. \label{eq:throughput}\end{equation} To ensure that the reconstructed frames, \( F_{\text{rec \), maintain fidelity, the structural similarity index measure (SSIM) or peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) must meet a minimum threshold: \begin{equation}Q_{\text{rec}}(F_{\text{rec}}) \geq Q_{\text{min\_rec}}.\end{equation}.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-238: The proposed method employs an encoder-decoder architecture optimized for V2I communication between a CAV and an MEC server, leveraging RFs distributed on both ends.
elements.sent-2411-239: On the CAV side (sender), we use an RF-encoder as depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:video_compression_rf_av ~a).}
elements.sent-2411-240: The CAV is equipped with multiple onboard cameras that continuously capture the surrounding environment in real-time as CAV-frames.
elements.sent-2411-241: Concurrently, a pretrained RF model generates RF-frames from the same camera poses, which represent a static environment devoid of dynamic or temporary objects, such as vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-242: For each camera pose, we obtain a pair of frames: a CAV-frame reflecting the current traffic conditions and an RF-frame representing the static environment.
elements.sent-2411-243: To achieve this, our RF-encoder first extract differences between image pairs to generate Delta-frames.
elements.sent-2411-244: This is done using a DS algorithm to process the CAV-frame and RF-frame pairs, producing Delta-frames that are encoded using a standard video codecs.
elements.sent-2411-245: Details of the DS method are provided in the following subsection Delta Segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-246: The distributed RF model serves as the I-frame (a complete frame) generator.
elements.sent-2411-247: Thus I-frame does not have to be transmitted, allowing only data about novel objects present in CAV-frame along with the corresponding camera poses to be transmitted to the MEC server.
elements.sent-2411-248: Upon receiving the encoded Delta-frames, the server decodes them and merges these Delta-frames with the corresponding RF-frames to reconstruct the original CAV-frames, called Rec-frames.
elements.sent-2411-249: Advanced DL techniques are then applied to the Rec-frames to perform downstream tasks such as object detection, semantic segmentation or advanced scene understanding.
elements.sent-2411-250: In the RFEncoder procedure, lines $1\text{- 11$ iterate over each frame in CAV\_Frames, where the camera pose is extracted, RF-frame is rendered based on the pose, and the Delta-frame is computed by comparing CAV-frame and RF-frame.}
elements.sent-2411-251: These computed Delta-frames are accumulated in a list and subsequently encoded.
elements.sent-2411-252: The ChannelTX procedure, detailed in lines $12\text{- 19$, segments the encoded deltas for transmission according to the available network throughput, with realistic packet loss.}
elements.sent-2411-253: Finally, the RFDecoder procedure, represented by lines $20\text{- 31$, receives the transmitted deltas, decodes them, and reconstructs each frame by merging the decoded Delta-frame with a freshly rendered RF-frame aligned to the transmitted pose, preserving frame integrity upon reception.}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{RF-Encoder, Network Transmission, and RF-Decoder} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Procedure{RFEncoder}{$\mathbf{B}_{\text{cav}}, \Psi_{\text{rf}}$} \State $\mathbf{B}_{\Delta} \gets \text{empty list}$ \For{$F_{\text{cav}} \in \mathbf{B}_{\text{cav}}$} \State $\pi \gets \text{ExtractPose} (F_{\text{cav}})$ \Comment{Get camera pose} \State $F_{\text{rf}} \gets \text{RenderViewFromRF}(\Psi_{\text{rf}}, \pi)$ \State $F_{\Delta} \gets \text{SegDelta}(F_{\text{cav}}, F_{\text{rf}})$ \Comment{Extract frame differences} \State $\text{Append}(\mathbf{B}_{\Delta}, (\pi, F_{\Delta}))$ \EndFor \State $\Delta \gets \text{EncDelta}(\mathbf{B}_{\Delta})$ \Comment{Compress Delta-frames} \State return $\Delta$ \EndProcedure \label{alg:rf_encoder} \Procedure{ChannelTX}{$\Delta, \text{Th}$} \State $\mathbf{\Delta}_{tx} \gets \text{empty list}$ \State $\text{size}_{\Delta} \gets \text{GetSize}(\Delta)$ \State $\tau \gets \text{size}_{\Delta} / \text{Th}$ \Comment{Estimate transmission time} \State $\text{TransmitOverChannel}(\Delta, \tau)$ \State $\text{Append}(\mathbf{\Delta}_{tx}, (\Delta, \tau))$ \State return $\mathbf{\Delta}_{tx}$ \EndProcedure \label{alg:network_tx} \Procedure{RFDecoder}{$\mathbf{\Delta}_{tx}, \Psi_{\text{rf}}$} \State $\mathbf{B}_{rec} \gets \text{empty list}$ \State ($\Delta, \tau) \gets \text{GetData}(\mathbf{\Delta}_{tx})$ \State $\text{WaitForTransmission}(\tau)$ \State $\mathbf{B}_{\Delta} \gets \text{DecDelta}(\Delta)$ \Comment{Decompress Delta-frames} \For{each $(\pi, F_{\Delta}) \in \mathbf{B}_{\Delta}$} \State $F_{\text{rf}} \gets \text{RenderViewFromRF}(\Psi_{\text{rf}}, \pi)$ \Comment{Render static view} \State $F_{\text{rec}} \gets \text{RecCAVFrame}(F_{\text{rf}}, F_{\Delta})$ \Comment{Reconstruct frame} \State $\text{Append}(\mathbf{B}_{rec}, F_{\text{rec}})$ \EndFor \State return $\mathbf{B}_{rec}$ \EndProcedure \label{alg:rf_decoder} \end{algorithmic} \label{alg:pseudocode_rfdvc}

elements.sent-2411-301: This work proposes an RFDVC approach, based on RF-encoder and RF-decoder architecture, for V2I communication and P2V synchronization within the vehicular metaverse.
elements.sent-2411-302: The experimental results show that RFDVC achieves significant data savings up to 71\% against H.264 codec and 45\% against H.265 codec in variety of conditions including lighting changes, and rain.

elements.sent-2411-310: This approach leverages a V2I communication between a CAV and a MEC server.
elements.sent-2411-311: RFs, distributed across the CAV (sender) and the MEC server (receiver), reconstruct static elements of an empty urban scene (e.g., buildings, roads or traffic lights), reducing the need for their repeated transmission.
elements.sent-2411-312: We segment differences, between CAV-frame capturing actual traffic and RF-frame capturing an empty scene from the same pose, represented as Delta-frames.
elements.sent-2411-313: The sender encodes and transmits the Delta-frames in batches over the MEC network, while the receiver reconstructs the CAV-frame as a Rec-frame by applying the Delta-frame to the corresponding RF-frame.
elements.sent-2411-314: RFDVC minimizes redundant data by storing it in distributed RFs at both ends, while Delta-frames retain only differences between CAV-frame and RF-frame from the same camera pose.
elements.sent-2411-315: We present an RF-encoder and an RF-decoder architecture using distributed RFs as DTs for storing photorealistic 3D urban scenes in a compressed form.
elements.sent-2411-316: We propose delta segmentation (DS) algorithm for extraction of differences between CAV-frame and RF-frame pairs without being limited by a predefined set of classes using zero-shot generalization.
elements.sent-2411-317: We also guarantee the transmission of critical classes (e.g., pedestrians or vehicles) using semantic segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-318: We validate the data compression achieved by the RFDVC framework compared to H.264 and H.265 codecs under different conditions as lighting changes, and rain.
elements.sent-2411-319: We also evaluate its performance under simulated packet loss to assess quality under challenging network conditions.
elements.sent-2411-320: In this section, we present the foundational principles of the H.264 and H.265 codecs widely utilized for real-time V2I communication to achieve efficient video compression via inter-frame and intra-frame encoding techniques.

elements.sent-2411-361: In this section, we propose RFDVC, a method aimed at minimizing latency and optimizing network performance by transmitting only novel or critical data, thereby excluding redundant and repetitive information stored in distributed RFs as DTs at both ends.

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{RF-Encoder, Network Transmission, and RF-Decoder} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Procedure{RFEncoder}{$\Psi_{\text{rf}}, \mathbf{F}_{\text{cav}}$} \State $\mathbf{F}_{\Delta} \gets []$ \For{$F_{\text{cav}} \in \mathbf{F}_{\text{cav}}$} \State $\text{cam}_{\text{pose}} \gets \text{ExtractPose} (F_{\text{cav}})$ \Comment{Get camera pose} \State $F_{\text{rf}} \gets \text{RenderViewFromRF}(\Psi_{\text{rf}}, \text{cam}_{\text{pose}})$ \State $F_{\Delta} \gets \text{SegDelta}(F_{\text{rf}}, F_{\text{cav}})$ \Comment{Extract frame differences} \State $\text{Append}(\mathbf{F}_{\Delta}, (\text{cam}_{\text{pose}}, F_{\Delta}))$ \EndFor \State $\Delta \gets \text{EncDelta}(\mathbf{F}_{\Delta})$ \Comment{Encode Delta-frames} \State return $\Delta$ \EndProcedure \label{alg:rf_encoder} \Procedure{ChannelTX}{$\Delta, T_{\text{net}}$} \State $\mathbf{b}_{stream} \gets []$ \State $\text{size}_{\Delta} \gets \text{GetSize}(\Delta)$ \State $\tau \gets \text{size}_{\Delta} / T_{\text{net}}$ \Comment{Estimate transmission time} \State $\text{TransmitOverChannel}(\Delta, \tau)$ \State $\text{Append}(\mathbf{b}_{stream}, (\Delta, \tau))$ \State return $\mathbf{b}_{stream}$ \EndProcedure \label{alg:network_tx} \Procedure{RFDecoder}{$\mathbf{b}_{stream}, \Psi_{\text{rf}}$} \State $\mathbf{F}_{rec} \gets []$ \State ($\Delta, \tau) \gets \text{GetData}(\mathbf{b}_{stream})$ \State $\text{WaitForTransmission}(\tau)$ \State $\mathbf{F}_{\Delta} \gets \text{DecDelta}(\Delta)$ \Comment{Decode Delta-frames} \For{each $(\text{cam}_{\text{pose}}, F_{\Delta}) \in \mathbf{F}_{\Delta}$} \State $F_{\text{rf}} \gets \text{RenderViewFromRF}(\Psi_{\text{rf}}, \text{cam}_{\text{pose}})$ \Comment{Render static view} \State $F_{\text{rec}} \gets \text{RecCAVFrame}(F_{\text{rf}}, F_{\Delta})$ \Comment{Reconstruct Rec-frame} \State $\text{Append}(\mathbf{F}_{rec}, F_{\text{rec}})$ \EndFor \State return $\mathbf{F}_{rec}$ \EndProcedure \label{alg:rf_decoder} \end{algorithmic} \label{alg:pseudocode_rfdvc}

elements.figure-2411-040: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figures/bler_vs_ssim_trends_2025_09_30_14_51_04.pdf} \caption{Impact of rainy weather on frame reconstruction quality (SSIM) under simulated packet loss for RFDVC and VC approaches. The evaluation includes encoding with H.264 and H.265 codecs and highlights the performance resilience of RFDVC under challenging conditions caused by rainfall.} \label{fig:ssim_bler_rain}
elements.sent-2411-454: Fig.~\ref{fig:ssim_bler_rain isolates the analysis to rainy weather conditions, showing a more pronounced SSIM degradation across all methods compared to the average results in Fig.~\ref{fig:ssim_bler_3weathers .}}
elements.sent-2411-455: The H.264-based RFDVC still demonstrates a clear edge in quality over its standard VC counterpart.
elements.sent-2411-456: At a BLER of approximately 0.25, H.264 RFDVC achieves an SSIM of $\sim0.55$, which is substantially higher than the standard H.264 VC's SSIM of $\sim0.37$ (+0.18 improvement).
elements.sent-2411-457: However, the challenging conditions of heavy rain diminish the effectiveness of the H.265-based RFDVC, which at the same BLER ($\sim0.30$ SSIM) underperforms relative to the standard H.265 VC ($\sim0.43$ SSIM).
elements.sent-2411-458: This suggests that severe visual artifacts from rain can complicate the delta segmentation process, making the standard codec more effective in this specific scenario.
elements.sent-2411-459: These findings affirm that the H.264-based RFDVC consistently delivers superior performance across all tested conditions.
elements.sent-2411-460: In contrast, while the more efficient H.265 RFDVC performs well with low packet loss, its visual quality degrades significantly as the error rate increases, underperforming relative to standard VC methods at BLER values above approximately 0.1 in both rainy and non-rainy conditions.
elements.sent-2411-461: This can be explained by the inherent nature of H.265, which contains less redundant information to achieve larger compression ratios, diminishing its resilience to packet damage.
elements.sent-2411-462: The experimental results show that RFDVC achieves significant data savings up to 71\% against H.264 codec and 44\% against H.265 codec in variety of conditions including lighting changes, and rain.
elements.sent-2411-463: RFDVC exhibits greater resilience to transmission errors, outperforming its standard counterpart in non-rainy conditions with up to a +0.26 SSIM improvement (H.264 RFDVC vs. H.264 VC) and maintaining a robust +0.18 improvement in rainy conditions, with both results at a BLER of 0.25.
elements.sent-2411-464: This resilience is further highlighted by the +0.29 SSIM performance gap observed between the H.264 and H.265 RFDVC variants under the non-rainy scenario.\par Future research will prioritize the processing of downstream tasks in advance within the vehicular metaverse, alongside adaptive updates to RFs as required.
